skill.name: mastra
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through Mastra framework development, including project setup, documentation lookup, agents, workflows, tools, memory, and migration checks.
skill.summary: Build and troubleshoot Mastra agents and workflows with current documentation checks.
skill.author: mastra-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and secret-related patterns, but review found them in Markdown setup and troubleshooting examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it guides package installation, project file creation, local documentation reads, remote documentation fetches, and API key configuration.

medium_findings.title: Project Setup Commands and Package Installation
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends commands such as npm create, npm install, npm run dev, npx codemod, mkdir, and docker run. These are legitimate Mastra development steps, but they can modify the workspace, install third-party packages, start services, or run package scripts, so users should approve commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in Markdown examples and are useful for the stated framework workflow. Confidence is high that they are operationally risky but not malicious because no hidden execution path exists in the skill files.

medium_findings.title: API Key and Environment File Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to create .env files and configure provider API keys for Mastra projects. This is expected for LLM applications, but it increases secret-handling risk if assistants echo keys, commit .env files, or transmit credentials while fetching documentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly mention .env files, API key names, and process.env usage. The examples do not exfiltrate secrets, so the finding is a medium-risk handling concern rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Remote Documentation Fetching
medium_findings.description: The skill directs assistants to use Mastra documentation URLs and WebFetch for current API information. These requests are to official documentation endpoints, but the behavior still requires network access and could expose query prompts to the documentation service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network guidance is explicit and repeated, with WebFetch examples limited to mastra.ai documentation. Confidence is high for legitimate network use and medium for user-data exposure risk because prompt contents depend on future use.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most reported Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, hardcoded IP, path traversal, and reconnaissance patterns are Markdown code fences, filenames, model names, localhost examples, or troubleshooting text. No executable Ruby, shell wrapper, obfuscated payload, or malicious control flow was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner matched documentation syntax and example text rather than executable source files. Confidence is high because all reviewed hits are in Markdown references and no separate scripts are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell commands for checking local packages, installing dependencies, running development servers, Docker setup, and migration tools. These are not automatically executed by the skill, but assistants using the skill may propose or run them during a user task.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are clear and numerous, but they appear inside Markdown guidance. The risk depends on whether an assistant executes them with user approval.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Reads and Project File Creation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs assistants to inspect node_modules documentation and create project files such as package.json, tsconfig.json, .env, and source files. This is normal for coding assistance but should stay inside the intended project workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file access instructions are explicit and legitimate for API verification and project scaffolding. Confidence is lower than command execution because actual impact depends on user-selected paths.

content.value_statement: Mastra APIs change quickly, so assistants can produce outdated code. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to verify current docs before building agents, workflows, tools, and migrations.
actual_capabilities: Finds installed Mastra package documentation in node_modules when available.
Uses official Mastra remote documentation when local docs are unavailable.
Guides creation of TypeScript Mastra projects with agents, tools, and workflows.
Explains common Mastra errors and version mismatch fixes.
Supports migration planning with current API checks and codemod guidance.
Clarifies model naming, memory, storage, RAG, and tool patterns.
limitations: Does not execute commands by itself; the assistant or user must run them.
Remote documentation may differ from the version installed in a project.
Examples require valid provider API keys supplied by the user.
It does not replace project-specific security review or production testing.

use_cases.title: Start a Mastra Project
use_cases.description: Create a TypeScript Mastra project with dependencies, configuration, an example tool, and a testable agent.

use_cases.title: Fix API Version Errors
use_cases.description: Resolve TypeScript and constructor errors by checking embedded package docs before changing code.

use_cases.title: Plan a Mastra Migration
use_cases.description: Review official migration notes, update packages together, verify new APIs, and test the upgraded application.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Mastra skill to create a minimal TypeScript Mastra project with one simple agent and one tool. Ask before running install commands.

prompt_templates.title: Verify an API Signature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Mastra embedded documentation workflow to verify the current Agent constructor signature in this project before editing code.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Workflow Error
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Mastra common errors guide to diagnose this workflow failure. Check whether the workflow is committed and whether step state is handled correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Version Upgrade
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Mastra migration guidance to plan an upgrade from my current version to the latest version. Include docs to check, commands to approve, and tests to run.

output_examples.input: Build a new Mastra weather agent.
output: A short setup plan with commands that require approval.
A TypeScript project structure for the agent, tool, and Mastra entry point.
A reminder to store provider keys in an environment file and avoid committing it.

output_examples.input: My Agent constructor code no longer type checks.
output: A diagnosis that version drift may be the cause.
Steps to inspect embedded docs in the installed @mastra packages.
A focused change list based on the verified local type definition.

output_examples.input: Upgrade my Mastra app to a newer major version.
output: A migration checklist covering official docs, dependency updates, codemods, API verification, and tests.
Warnings about updating all @mastra packages together.
Post-upgrade checks for TypeScript, runtime behavior, and Studio startup.

best_practices: Check embedded docs first when Mastra packages are installed in the project.
Ask for approval before installing packages, running codemods, or starting services.
Keep API keys in local environment files and never include them in shared output.
anti_patterns: Writing Mastra code from memory without checking current documentation.
Using remote docs as the only source when local package docs are available.
Committing .env files or provider API keys into source control.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants build and troubleshoot Mastra applications using current local or official documentation.

faq.question: Does it create projects automatically?
faq.answer: It provides project setup steps and examples. Commands and file changes still need user approval in normal coding workflows.

faq.question: Why does it check node_modules?
faq.answer: Mastra APIs change often. Installed package docs show the exact API version used by the current project.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Network access is useful when local docs are unavailable or when checking official Mastra documentation.

faq.question: How should API keys be handled?
faq.answer: Store keys in local environment files or secret managers. Do not paste real keys into public prompts or committed files.

faq.question: Can it help with migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides migration planning, dependency updates, codemod checks, API verification, and post-upgrade testing.
